title: Guajava suffruticosa
intro: Guajava suffruticosa, also known as the small guava or Chilean guava, is a shrub species in the family Myrtaceae, native to South America. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, which may help alleviate digestive issues, reduce inflammation, and support immune function. The bioactive constituents of Guajava suffruticosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Traditional herbal preparations made from Guajava suffruticosa include teas, infusions, and tinctures, often used to treat various health conditions and promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Guajava suffruticosa, also known as the dwarf guava, is a shrub-like species within the Myrtaceae family. This family includes various plants such as guavas, eucalyptus, and clove trees. Guajava suffruticosa is native to tropical and subtropical regions and is closely related to other guava species like Guajava cinnamomifolia. The classification of Guajava suffruticosa is as follows: Kingdom - Plantae, Phylum - Magnoliophyta, Class - Magnoliopsida, Order - Myrtales, Family - Myrtaceae, Genus - Psidium, Species - suffruticosum. The specific classification may vary slightly depending on the source, but it generally falls within the Myrtaceae family.
uses: Guajava suffruticosa, also known as the dwarf guava, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. Its leaves, bark, and roots have been used to treat ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant's leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, making them effective in treating wounds and infections. In some parts of the world, the plant is also used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. Further research is needed to confirm the full extent of the plant's medicinal uses and potential health benefits.

side_effects: Guajava suffruticosa, commonly known as the guava shrub, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, but like any other plant, it can cause side effects. Consuming excessive amounts of the plant's extract or leaves may lead to gastrointestinal issues such as nausea and diarrhea. Allergic reactions to the plant's sap or leaves can also occur, resulting in skin irritation and itching. Furthermore, high doses of the plant's extract may interact with certain medications and exacerbate underlying health conditions. As with any herbal remedy, it is essential to consult a healthcare professional before using Guajava suffruticosa for medicinal purposes.
